explanation: The Sigma rule relies on exact substring matching for 'python -h'. By explicitly including the file extension (python.exe), the exact string 'python -h' is no longer present in the command line, bypassing the detection.
The rule expects exactly one space between the command and the argument. Adding double or multiple spaces (e.g., 'python  -h') breaks the strict string 'contains' condition.
An attacker can verify the presence of the tool by checking its version (-V) rather than invoking the help menu (-h or --help). The rule currently only flags help-related arguments.

In Windows environments, using caret (^) escape characters obfuscates the command string. Cmd.exe interprets and strips the carets to run Python, but the logged command line will not trigger the static string match.
Similar to the Python bypass, appending the .exe extension to the executable name prevents the exact substring 'wget --help' from matching.
